nativeRange.habitat: Carnation Tree Corals inhabit reef walls, overhangs, cave entrances, and shaded ledges swept by strong tidal currents. Colonies tend to be partially or fully retracted during daylight hours and extend their polyps fully at night to filter feed from the water column.

decorPreferences: areas of strong non-laminar or alternating flow
position away from direct intense lighting

diet.notes: Dendronephthya is entirely non-photosynthetic — it contains no zooxanthellae (the symbiotic algae that power most reef corals) and derives no energy from light whatsoever. Survival depends entirely on continuous filter feeding from the water column. Diet should consist of live or high-quality preserved phytoplankton (Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, or Isochrysis; particle size roughly 3–20 µm), supplemented with copepod nauplii, rotifers, marine snow, and other micro-zooplankton. Live phytoplankton is generally considered more effective than preserved formulations. Food must be available almost constantly in the water column — pulsed several times daily at minimum — with dedicated non-photosynthetic (NPS) dosing systems or refugium-based live cultures commonly recommended for reliable supply. Baby brine shrimp and cyclop-eeze have also been used as supplemental foods. Regular addition of iodine, strontium, and other trace elements is generally advised.

goodWith: gorgonians and other NPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles
crinoids (known associates that may stress the colony)

summary: The Carnation Tree Coral (Dendronephthya sp.) is a vividly colored, non-photosynthetic soft coral that derives no nutrition from light and must be fed continuously with live plankton. It is widely considered one of the most difficult corals in the hobby, with most captive specimens declining within months even under expert care.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT: The Carnation Tree Coral is widely considered one of the most difficult corals ever offered in the marine aquarium hobby. Captive mortality is extremely high — most wild-collected specimens decline and die within weeks to months, even under expert care. Purchase is strongly discouraged unless the keeper has a fully dedicated non-photosynthetic (NPS) system and has already successfully maintained simpler NPS species such as Scleronephthya.

Non-photosynthetic biology: Unlike the vast majority of reef aquarium corals, Dendronephthya contains no zooxanthellae — the symbiotic algae that allow most reef corals to feed through photosynthesis. Lighting plays no role in its nutrition, and high-intensity lighting is unnecessary and may be harmful. These corals tend to open more fully in dim conditions and are most active at night.

Feeding: Feeding failure is the single most commonly cited cause of death in captive Dendronephthya. Food must be present in the water column nearly continuously — dosing once per day is generally considered insufficient. The recommended approach involves live phytoplankton cultures (Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, or Isochrysis), supplied several times daily, supplemented with rotifers and copepod nauplii. Pulse-dosing of prepared liquid coral foods alone has generally proven insufficient for long-term survival.

Flow: Alternating, non-laminar (bidirectional, tidal-type) moderate-to-strong flow is generally considered optimal, mimicking the natural surge of reef environments. Flow velocities around 15–20 cm/s have been cited as effective. Direct, constant laminar flow may damage polyps. Strong water movement is essential for food delivery and waste removal — polyps are generally unable to feed adequately without it.

Lighting: Low (50–150 PAR). High-intensity light is neither required nor beneficial. Placing the colony in a shaded area, under an overhang, or in a dimmer zone of the rockwork is generally advised. High-intensity lighting may cause tissue retraction or stress.

System maturity: A fully cycled, mature reef system — at minimum 12 months old — with established live rock, diverse microfauna, and stable water chemistry is generally considered a prerequisite. Some researchers have suggested that nutritional contributions from fish waste, sponge metabolites, or other complex reef community byproducts may play an important role, which may explain why even well-fed specimens in newer or simpler systems often fail.

Water quality: Pristine, highly stable parameters are required. Protein skimming, RO/DI top-off water, and regular partial water changes all help maintain the water quality this species demands. Regular supplementation with iodine, strontium, and other trace elements is generally recommended.

Sizing and growth: In the wild, colonies can reach 1–2 meters in height. Under the rare successful captive conditions that have been documented, growth rates of roughly 30 cm over a few months have been reported. Captive colonies are typically sold as small fragments (10–20 cm) attached to rubble or live rock.

Dedicated NPS system: Expert opinion consistently holds that Dendronephthya should only be attempted in a purpose-built NPS (non-photosynthetic) reef tank. Housing it alongside photosynthetic corals that require high-PAR lighting creates an inherent conflict, since the intense light that benefits those corals may stress or harm Dendronephthya. A dedicated NPS tank with dimmed or no lighting, continuous phytoplankton dosing infrastructure, and mature live rock is the approach most consistently associated with long-term survival.

Color variants: The Carnation Tree Coral is available in brilliant reds, oranges, pinks, purples, and yellows. Red and orange forms marketed under the trade name 'Strawberry Coral' are among those most commonly encountered for sale. Color is produced by calcareous sclerites (tiny mineral spicules) embedded in the tissue rather than by photopigments, so coloration does not fade through bleaching the way photosynthetic corals do — but it does fade as the animal's health deteriorates.

Species note: Dendronephthya is a large genus of 250 or more described species. The aquarium trade commonly sells multiple species under the umbrella names 'Carnation Coral' or 'Tree Coral', with D. klunzingeri and D. rubeola among the most frequently discussed. Husbandry requirements are generally considered identical across the species in this genus.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in captivity has not been documented. Sexual reproduction occurs in the wild; asexual fragmentation is theoretically possible but is not practiced in the aquarium trade due to the extreme baseline difficulty of keeping this genus alive. Fragging is not recommended.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – Carnation Corals in the Home Aquarium
additional.notes: Detailed NPS husbandry article. Key data: optimal flow 15–20 cm/s bidirectional laminar; phytoplankton diet 5,000–50,000 cells/mL; colony height up to 1 m with foot/month growth rates documented; live phyto preferred over preserved; bacterial infection implicated as common cause of death. Cross-checked feeding methodology and flow requirements.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Carnation Tree Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.5°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level expert, flow strong, placement anywhere, feeding multiple daily. Native range: South Pacific. Good agreement with LiveAquaria on all key parameters.

additional.name: Reeflings – Dendronephthya klunzingeri Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting 50–100 PAR, temperature 24–26°C, salinity 1.023–1.026, placement in shaded areas. Minor disagreement on temperature upper bound (26°C vs 25.5°C from other sources — averaged to 26°C max). Minor disagreement on PAR range — ReefChasers listed 100–150 PAR while Reeflings listed 50–100; final range of 50–150 adopted.

additional.name: Subdiversion – Dendronephthya spp. species profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indian Ocean, Red Sea, Pacific), depth range 3–45 m, colony size 1–2 m, taxonomy (Family Nephtheidae), nocturnal filter-feeding behavior. Confirmed natural predators include Dermatobranchus and Armina nudibranchs, coral crabs, spider crabs, cowries, and crinoids.

additional.name: Reef Builders – NPS Coral Tank Size Considerations
additional.notes: Expert NPS practitioner recommends ~50 gallons (~200 L) with large sump as practical minimum for NPS reef systems; nano setups possible but require extreme maintenance regimens. Adopted 200 L as minimum tank volume.
